title: Strong ballistic transport for quasiperiodic Schrodinger operators and Lieb-Robinson bounds for XY spin chains
speaker: Ilya Kachkovskiy
abstract: I will discuss some results and open questions related to transport properties of 1D quasiperiodic operators with absolutely continuous spectra, and their relations to integrable many-body systems. Most of the results will be based on https://arxiv.org/abs/1505.07150

proof_stages.label: XY-chain transport and Lieb--Robinson velocity regimes
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the isotropic XY spin chain, local observables, and the Lieb--Robinson commutator bound, then frames the main question as determining whether the admissible velocities are zero, arbitrarily small but nonzero, or bounded below by a positive speed.

proof_stages.label: Jordan--Wigner reduction to one-particle Schrödinger dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The Jordan--Wigner transform produces special observables whose Heisenberg evolution is governed by a finite discrete Schrödinger operator, so uniform spin-chain transport information is encoded in a whole-line one-particle operator, though the transform is nonlocal.

proof_stages.label: Strong ballistic transport as the needed one-particle notion
proof_stages.summary: For the whole-line Schrödinger operator, transport is measured by growth of the position observable |X(t)|; Cesàro or merely nonaveraged lower bounds are too weak for the spin-chain application, motivating strong convergence X(t)/t to an asymptotic velocity operator Q with trivial kernel, which yields a positive Lieb--Robinson velocity lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic velocity as averaged current and diagonal truncation
proof_stages.summary: Using the Newton--Leibniz formula, X(t)/t is reduced modulo a vanishing term to the long-time Cesàro average of the bounded current A=i[H,X], and in a pure point spectral representation this average keeps only the diagonal blocks P_j A P_j.

proof_stages.label: Floquet-fiber computation for periodic operators
proof_stages.summary: Periodic Schrödinger or Jacobi operators decompose into finite Floquet matrices, allowing the pure-point diagonal calculation to be done on each fiber and then direct-integrated, giving an asymptotic velocity related to derivatives of band eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Quasiperiodic AC regime and Aubry duality
proof_stages.summary: For analytic quasiperiodic potentials, the relevant small-coupling/Diophantine regime has absolutely continuous spectrum and reducibility, while Aubry duality identifies the direct-integral family with a dual family whose fibers have point spectrum under the corresponding localization result.

proof_stages.label: Dual-fiber computation of quasiperiodic strong ballistic transport
proof_stages.summary: The operator Q is computed after passing to the Aubry-dual direct integral, applying the point-spectrum fiber calculation, and transforming back, yielding almost-everywhere strong ballistic transport along a subsequence of times and hence a lower bound on Lieb--Robinson velocities.

proof_stages.label: Abstract criterion outlook
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes by emphasizing the open problem of characterizing, without reducing to point-spectrum fibers, which absolutely continuous Schrödinger operators admit the asymptotic-velocity limit, perhaps through reflectionless or homogenization-type structure.

examples.name: Fibonacci Hamiltonian potential

examples.name: Random magnetic field and Anderson localization
examples.note: Random i.i.d. magnetic fields are cited as a standard situation where the one-particle Schrödinger operator has pure point spectrum and the spin-chain Lieb-Robinson velocity can be zero in suitable formulations.
examples.engagement_reason: random magnetic fields are cited as a case where the associated Schrödinger operator has pure point spectrum and zero-velocity behavior, with results attributed to others but not derived

passes.reason: The random-field Anderson-localized case realizes the zero-velocity/no-transport extreme of the Lieb-Robinson regimes, as an illustrative regime.
